source: Отчет о внедрении логистической платформы Титан, июнь 2026

text.value: 1. Executive overview. Meridian Retail Group launched the Titan logistics program in June 2026. The largest rollout target is the Novaya Dacha distribution center near Minsk. The program combines warehouse execution, yard scheduling, robot sorting, and demand forecasting in one operating playbook.

2. Warehouse systems. The Novaya Dacha distribution center uses Sokol WMS as the primary warehouse management system and Talon Yard for dock appointments. Sokol WMS owns inventory reservations while Talon Yard assigns gates and trailer arrival windows.

3. Automation lane. The Phoenix-12 robot sorting line was installed in aisle C of the Novaya Dacha distribution center. Phoenix-12 receives carton waves from Sokol WMS and sends blocked-lane alerts back to Talon Yard when dock gate 7 is congested.

4. Cold-chain customer. Orion Foods ships chilled dairy pallets to the Novaya Dacha distribution center three times per week. The contract requires a four-hour unloading SLA; missed windows increase spoilage risk and trigger an exception review.

5. Incident KR-42. During the second pilot night, Sokol WMS stopped receiving confirmations from mobile scanners. Night shift Bravo handled the incident KR-42, switched two inbound lanes to manual confirmation, and kept Phoenix-12 running at reduced speed until the scanner queue recovered.

6. Forecasting loop. Helios Forecast feeds weekly replenishment windows into Sokol WMS for Meridian Retail Group. The forecast service also marks probable returns from Orion Foods so the warehouse can reserve chilled staging space before the truck arrives.

7. Safety constraint. Phoenix-12 allows a manual override only after Talon Yard confirms gate 7 is closed. The Novaya Dacha operating manual names this as the safety interlock between the robot line and yard control.

8. Pilot outcome. After two weeks, Meridian Retail Group reported that Novaya Dacha reduced trailer dwell time by eighteen percent and cleared the Orion Foods backlog without additional weekend labor. The next rollout candidate is the Temirtau cross-dock hub.

Appendix C — extended pilot operations log for the Titan logistics program at the Novaya Dacha distribution center, compiled by the Meridian Retail Group program office in June 2026. The extended pilot ran for fourteen consecutive nights and was designed to stress every subsystem at once rather than in isolation. Day one opened with a full inventory reconciliation between Sokol WMS and the legacy spreadsheet ledger, and the two systems agreed on ninety-eight percent of stock-keeping units before the first inbound wave arrived. The yard team used Talon Yard to sequence eleven trailers across three dock doors, and the average gate turnaround dropped to twenty-two minutes once the appointment windows were staggered by fifteen minutes. On the automation lane, the Phoenix-12 robot sorting line processed its first four thousand cartons without a single jam, and the maintenance crew logged only routine belt-tension checks. Helios Forecast published a revised replenishment plan that pulled two chilled Orion Foods deliveries forward by a day to protect against a forecasted heat wave. The forecasting team noted that pulling the deliveries forward reduced the risk of a cold-chain exception during the hottest part of the week. On the second pilot night the operation hit its first serious obstacle. The Sokol WMS scanner outage during incident KR-42 was traced to a misconfigured VLAN 318 on switch stack DL-7, and the fix was a firmware rollback to build R429. Before the network team isolated the fault, handheld scanners across two zones stopped acknowledging pallet scans, and the confirmation queue began to back up within minutes. While the network team worked the root cause, Night shift Bravo switched two inbound lanes to manual confirmation and kept Phoenix-12 running at reduced speed so the sortation queue never fully stalled. Manual confirmation added roughly forty seconds per pallet, but it preserved the four-hour unloading SLA that the Orion Foods contract requires. Once switch stack DL-7 was rebooted and the firmware rollback to build R429 took effect, scanner confirmations resumed and the manual lanes were retired within the hour. A short post-incident review recommended pinning the switch firmware and adding a VLAN validation step to the nightly network checklist. In recognition of the recovery, the program office approved a special award. Night shift Bravo received a retention bonus of twelve thousand rubles per operator for clearing the Orion Foods backlog without weekend overtime. The finance note recorded the bonus as a one-time pilot incentive rather than a recurring line item, and it flagged the payment for review at the next budget cycle. Over the following week, trailer dwell time at Novaya Dacha fell by eighteen percent compared with the pre-pilot baseline, and the cold-chain exception rate for Orion Foods dropped below one percent. The safety review confirmed that the interlock between Phoenix-12 and Talon Yard behaved correctly during every gate-seven closure, releasing the manual override only after yard control acknowledged the closed gate. Maintenance replaced two worn diverter arms on the Phoenix-12 line and recalibrated the induction scanner without pausing the overnight run. The demand-planning group compared Helios Forecast projections against actuals and found the weekly replenishment error stayed under four percent for every category except seasonal produce. Sokol WMS logged a single reservation conflict when two pickers grabbed the same tote, and the exception was cleared by a supervisor override in under a minute. The yard team tested a contingency plan for a blocked dock gate and confirmed that Talon Yard could reroute an inbound trailer to a neighboring door without a manual phone call. A separate power-quality audit on night nine found a marginal voltage sag on the induction feeder, and facilities scheduled a transformer tap adjustment before the next quarter. The training coordinator ran two refresher sessions on the manual confirmation procedure so every shift could repeat what Night shift Bravo did without hesitation. Procurement confirmed that spare switch stacks and a matching firmware image would be kept on site so a future outage like KR-42 could be resolved in minutes rather than hours. The sustainability team measured a small drop in idle-conveyor energy after the diverter arms were replaced and logged it as an unexpected side benefit of routine maintenance. On the final night, the program office ran a full end-to-end rehearsal from trailer arrival through robot sortation to outbound staging and recorded no blocking defects. The closing summary recommended replicating the Sokol WMS, Talon Yard, Helios Forecast, and Phoenix-12 configuration at the Temirtau cross-dock hub, and it treated the KR-42 network fix and the Bravo retention bonus as documented precedents for the next rollout. The appendix ends with a reminder that the Novaya Dacha operating manual, not this log, remains the authoritative source for every interlock and escalation procedure.
